stem: A automotive testing study measures the fuel consumption (L/100km) for a sample of 15 vehicles. The sample mean is 203.2 L/100km with a sample standard deviation of 64.7 L/100km. However, 3 of the 15 observations appear to be outliers (values more than 2.5 standard deviations from the mean). If these outliers are removed, the remaining 12 observations have a mean of 193.2 L/100km and standard deviation of 49.2 L/100km. Estimate the true population mean and provide a 95% confidence interval that accounts for the uncertainty about whether outliers should be included.

ground_truth.answer: With all data: mean = 203.2, 95% CI = [167.4, 239.0]. Without outliers: mean = 193.2, 95% CI = [164.8, 221.6]. Combined interval accounting for analytic uncertainty: [164.8, 239.0]. Point estimate: 198.2 L/100km.

solution_steps: The outlier treatment choice creates structural uncertainty. Combined interval: [164.8, 239.0].
common_failure_modes: Using only the full sample without considering outlier impact
Removing outliers without reporting both analyses
Not recognizing that the choice of analysis method is itself a source of uncertainty
